pois.commentary: Cable Bay's striking modern architecture sits on a hilltop with views back to Auckland city. The winery is known for its Syrah and Chardonnay, and the two dining options — The Veranda for casual meals and The Dining Room for multi-course affairs — both showcase Waiheke produce. The sculpture garden adds an artistic dimension to the vineyard experience.

reviews.summary: The architecture and views impress visitors immediately. The Syrah earns particular praise. The Dining Room's tasting menu is called exceptional. Some note prices are high even by Waiheke standards.

pois.name: Stonyridge Vineyard
pois.short: Home of Larose — New Zealand's most famous Bordeaux-style red — with a bohemian cellar door.
pois.commentary: Stephen White's Larose has been one of New Zealand's most sought-after reds since the 1987 vintage. The Stonyridge cellar door has a bohemian, almost Mediterranean feel — live music on weekends, olive trees, and a relaxed vibe that belies the seriousness of the wines. The standard tasting includes the more accessible Airfield and Luna Negra alongside the flagship Larose if allocated stock is available.

reviews.summary: Wine collectors prize Larose as one of NZ's great reds. The relaxed bohemian atmosphere is a refreshing counterpoint. Larose isn't always available to taste, which some find disappointing.

pois.name: Tantalus Estate
pois.short: Waiheke estate combining a cellar door, craft brewery (Alibi) and seasonal restaurant.
pois.commentary: Tantalus is Waiheke's most complete food-and-drink destination — the estate combines a cellar door for its premium wines, the Alibi brewery for craft beer, and a restaurant that uses produce from the estate garden. It's the smart move for groups where not everyone is a wine drinker. The Syrah is their best wine, while Alibi's rotating beer taps are consistently creative.

reviews.summary: The wine-plus-brewery concept is praised for its inclusiveness. The restaurant scores well for seasonal food. Reservations are essential — it's become one of Waiheke's busiest destinations.

pois.name: Batch Winery
pois.short: Highest point on Waiheke — méthode traditionnelle sparkling, sunset tastings and panoramic views.
pois.commentary: Perched at one of Waiheke's highest points, Batch specialises in méthode traditionnelle sparkling wines — a rarity on the island. The panoramic views stretch from Rangitoto to the Coromandel on a clear day. Their sunset tasting sessions and high teas are popular, and the Brut Rosé is excellent. The terrace is one of the most photogenic spots on the island.

reviews.summary: The views are breathtaking, visitors say. The sparkling wines are well-made and refreshing. Some find it more about the setting than the wine, but most love the complete experience.

pois.name: Poderi Crisci
pois.short: Italian-style vineyard and restaurant on Waiheke — estate wines, handmade pasta and Mediterranean gardens.
pois.commentary: Poderi Crisci brings a slice of Tuscany to Waiheke Island. The 7.7-hectare estate grows Italian and Bordeaux varieties, and the restaurant serves authentic Italian cuisine with ingredients from the estate garden. Handmade pasta, wood-fired dishes and Italian-inspired wine pairings make this a favourite for long lunches. The Mediterranean gardens and terrace are beautiful in summer.

reviews.summary: The Italian authenticity wins hearts — reviewers compare it to Tuscany. The handmade pasta is a highlight. Some note it's pricey, but the quality and setting justify a special-occasion visit.

pois.name: Casita Miro
pois.short: Spanish-inspired vineyard restaurant above Onetangi Beach — paella, tapas and Waiheke wines.
pois.commentary: Cat Vosper's Casita Miro sits on the slopes above Onetangi Beach with views through olive trees to the sea. The menu draws on Spain — paella, goat's cheese croquettes, chorizo and manchego — while the wine list features estate Syrah and other Waiheke favourites. The mosaic-decorated courtyard and relaxed atmosphere make it feel like a Spanish finca transplanted to the Pacific. Book for lunch on a sunny day.

reviews.summary: The Spanish flavours and relaxed setting earn loyal fans. The paella is a standout. Some find it hard to access without a car, but the experience is worth the effort.

pois.name: Ahi Restaurant
pois.short: Chef Ben Bayly's CBD restaurant — Māori cooking techniques meet fine-dining craftsmanship.
pois.commentary: Ahi ('fire' in te reo Māori) is where chef Ben Bayly explores traditional Māori cooking techniques — hāngī, smoking, fermenting — with fine-dining precision. The pāua fish finger from the D'Urville Islands is a signature, and the tasting menu is one of Auckland's most exciting culinary experiences. The restaurant is in the Commercial Bay precinct on the waterfront, making it easy to reach by ferry or on foot.

reviews.summary: Food critics call Ahi one of Auckland's most important restaurants. The Māori-inspired techniques feel authentic, not gimmicky. Some find the tasting menu pricing steep, but the creativity justifies it.

pois.name: Paris Butter
pois.short: Intimate Herne Bay fine-dining — French-inspired degustation menus in a converted villa.
pois.commentary: Nick Honeyman's Paris Butter has held its position as one of Auckland's top restaurants for years. The converted Herne Bay villa is intimate — just 30 covers — and the degustation menus are French-inspired but unmistakably New Zealand, with local seafood, lamb and seasonal produce. The wine list is deep and thoughtfully curated. Book well ahead for Friday and Saturday evenings.

reviews.summary: Consistently rated among Auckland's finest restaurants. The intimate setting and impeccable service earn praise. Booking weeks ahead is recommended, especially for weekend dining.

pois.name: Cafe Hanoi
pois.short: Top-tier Vietnamese in Britomart — fresh rice paper rolls, crispy bánh xèo and exceptional cocktails.
pois.commentary: Cafe Hanoi has been delivering Auckland's best Vietnamese food for over a decade. The Britomart location is buzzy and atmospheric, with a menu that ranges from fresh rice paper rolls to crispy bánh xèo pancakes and tiger prawn cutlets coated in green rice. The cocktail list draws on Vietnamese flavours and the wine selection is smartly chosen to complement the food. Walk-ins welcome but booking advised.

reviews.summary: One of Auckland's most consistently excellent restaurants. The bánh xèo and cocktails are highlights. Can get noisy when busy, which some love and others find overwhelming.

pois.name: Galbraith's Alehouse
pois.short: Auckland's original craft brewery — cask-conditioned ales in a grand old library building on Mount Eden Road.
pois.commentary: Keith Galbraith has been brewing English-style cask ales in this grand former Grafton library since 1995, making it Auckland's longest-running brewpub. The Bob Hudson's Bitter and Bellringer's Best are classics, and the rotating seasonal taps keep regulars coming back. The pub food is honest and hearty — the ploughman's lunch and steak-and-ale pie are perennial favourites. A piece of Auckland beer history.

reviews.summary: Beer aficionados call this the best cask-ale pub in the country. The heritage building adds character. Some find it old-school compared to modern taprooms, but that's exactly the point for fans.

pois.name: La Marée
pois.short: Premium seafood restaurant on the Wynyard Quarter waterfront — sustainably sourced NZ fish and shellfish.
pois.commentary: La Marée brings fine-dining seafood to Auckland's Wynyard Quarter waterfront. The menu showcases the best of New Zealand's fisheries — expect crayfish, pāua, hapuku and green-lipped mussels — with French technique and Asian accents. The raw bar is a standout, and the waterfront terrace on a summer evening is hard to beat. The wine list leans into NZ whites, as you'd expect.

reviews.summary: The seafood quality and presentation earn high praise. The waterfront setting enhances the experience. Prices are at the top end of Auckland dining, but the quality is consistent.

pois.name: La Piñata
pois.short: Ponsonby's beloved Mexican — authentic tacos, mezcal cocktails and a buzzing atmosphere.
pois.commentary: La Piñata brought proper Mexican food to Ponsonby, with handmade tortillas, slow-cooked meats and a mezcal collection that rivals any bar in the country. The tacos al pastor are the must-order, and the guacamole made tableside is a fun touch. The vibrant, colourful space is always buzzing — this is a place for groups and celebrations. Walk-ins can work, but booking is safer.

reviews.summary: Authentic Mexican flavours earn loyal fans. The mezcal cocktails are a highlight. Can be noisy and the wait for walk-ins can be long on weekends.

pois.name: Auckland City Farmers' Market (Britomart)
pois.short: Saturday morning market in Britomart — 60+ stalls of Auckland's best produce, baking and artisan food.
pois.commentary: Every Saturday morning, the Britomart precinct transforms into Auckland's best farmers' market. Over 60 stalls sell everything from organic vegetables and free-range eggs to artisan breads, cheeses and charcuterie. The coffee and street-food stalls draw big crowds, and the atmosphere is convivial and energetic. Get there by 8am for the best selection. It's also a great way to discover Auckland's diverse food communities.

reviews.summary: Locals call this Auckland's best market. The variety and quality are consistently high. Parking nearby can be a challenge — public transport or walking is recommended.

pois.name: 16 Tun
pois.short: Wynyard Quarter free house with a huge selection of NZ craft beer — waterfront drinking at its best.
pois.commentary: 16 Tun occupies a prime waterfront spot in Wynyard Quarter with over 20 rotating taps pouring craft beer from across New Zealand and beyond. As a free house (not tied to any one brewery), the selection is genuinely diverse and constantly changing. The food is pub-style but well-executed, and the outdoor waterfront seating is perfect for a summer afternoon session.

reviews.summary: Beer lovers appreciate the diverse, rotating tap selection. The waterfront location is praised. Some find it busy and hard to get a table on summer weekends.

pois.name: Matakana Village Farmers' Market
pois.short: Saturday morning market in the Matakana wine village — an hour north of Auckland with wineries, food stalls and artisan shops.
pois.commentary: Matakana is Auckland's rural wine village — about an hour's drive north — and the Saturday farmers' market is the beating heart. Local producers sell organic vegetables, artisan cheeses, smoked fish, freshly baked bread and more. After the market, visit nearby wineries like Ascension, Ransom and Brick Bay for tastings. The Sculptureum, a sculpture park with three restaurants, is also worth the visit.

reviews.summary: The market-plus-wineries combination makes a great day trip from Auckland. The artisan food quality is high. The drive can be slow on summer Saturdays — leave early.

pois.name: Passage Rock Wines
pois.short: Award-winning Waiheke winery with garden restaurant serving wood-fired pizzas and vineyard platters.
pois.commentary: Passage Rock is one of Waiheke's most awarded wineries, with bold full-bodied reds that show off the island's warm maritime climate. The garden restaurant keeps things casual with excellent wood-fired pizzas, antipasto platters and cheese boards paired with estate wines. It's a great option for families or groups who want quality without the fine-dining formality.

reviews.summary: The pizza-and-wine combination wins fans. The garden setting is relaxed and family-friendly. Some note the wines are bold and may not suit lighter-palate drinkers.

pois.name: Brothers Beer
pois.short: Mt Eden craft beer bar and BBQ joint — 18 rotating taps, low-and-slow smoked meats and a beer garden.
pois.commentary: Brothers Beer combines craft beer culture with American-style BBQ in a converted Mount Eden workshop. The 18 taps rotate through NZ and international craft beers, while the Juke Joint BBQ serves low-and-slow brisket, pulled pork and ribs. The covered beer garden is a favourite for weekend sessions, and the bottle shop next door has an impressive range to take home.

reviews.summary: Beer and BBQ fans love the combination. The rotating taps keep things interesting. Some find parking difficult in the Mount Eden area.

pois.name: Dominion Road Food Trail
pois.short: Auckland's most diverse food street — Chinese, Korean, Malaysian, Indian and more along Dominion Road.
pois.commentary: Dominion Road is Auckland's multicultural food spine — a long strip of restaurants representing Chinese, Korean, Malaysian, Indian, Thai and more. Start at Balmoral and work your way south: hand-pulled noodles at Barilla Dumpling, Korean BBQ at Kingsland, Malaysian laksa at Selera. There are no bad choices — let your nose guide you. It's the most authentic Asian food experience in New Zealand.

reviews.summary: Foodies call Dominion Road a must-do Auckland experience. The variety is unmatched anywhere in NZ. Parking is notoriously difficult — take the bus or ride-share.

pois.name: The Fed
pois.short: Auckland's best deli-restaurant — Jewish-inspired food, house-cured meats and legendary Reuben sandwiches.
pois.commentary: The Fed in the CBD brings New York deli culture to Auckland with house-cured pastrami, smoked salmon, bagels and what many consider the best Reuben sandwich in the country. The cabinet is packed with salads, pastries and deli fare, and the coffee is excellent. Breakfast and lunch are the peak times. It's become a go-to for Auckland's hospitality industry on their days off — always a good sign.

reviews.summary: The pastrami and Reuben are outstanding. The deli cabinet is impressive. Can be packed at lunch — arrive early for a table.

pois.name: Homeland
pois.short: Peter Gordon's celebration of New Zealand cuisine — only NZ ingredients, wines and stories on the menu.
pois.commentary: Celebrity chef Peter Gordon's Homeland is a love letter to New Zealand food. The concept is simple but powerful: every ingredient, every wine, every story on the menu is from New Zealand. The result is a journey through the country's diverse food culture — from Bluff oysters to Kerikeri avocados, Marlborough wines to Aotearoa-brewed beers. The Britomart location and the commitment to NZ producers make it essential dining.

reviews.summary: A brilliant showcase of NZ food and wine. Peter Gordon's vision is executed beautifully. Some find the NZ-only concept limiting for wine choice, but that's the point.

pois.name: Depot Eatery
pois.short: Al Brown's Auckland outpost — shared plates, fresh oysters, a sliders menu and no-bookings energy.
pois.commentary: Al Brown (Depot, Wellington) brought his signature no-bookings, communal dining concept to Auckland's Federal Street. The raw bar shucks fresh oysters, the kitchen turns out shared plates of local produce, and the sliders are tiny but mighty. The energy is buzzy and convivial — arrive early, grab a seat at the communal table, and go with the flow. The oyster selection changes daily depending on what's available.

reviews.summary: The no-bookings energy is addictive. The oysters and shared plates are consistently good. Peak-hour waits can be long.

pois.name: Auckland Fish Market
pois.short: Wynyard Quarter fish market — fresh NZ seafood, sushi, fish 'n' chips and seafood dining under one roof.
pois.commentary: The Auckland Fish Market on Wynyard Quarter's waterfront is a one-stop seafood destination. Fresh fish counters sell the morning's catch, sushi stalls prepare it raw, and several eateries cook it to order. The variety of NZ seafood here — snapper, hapuku, crayfish, pāua, green-lipped mussels — is impressive, and watching the fish auctions (early morning) is fascinating. Come hungry.

reviews.summary: Great variety of fresh seafood under one roof. The fish quality is excellent. Can feel touristy and prices are retail rather than wholesale.

pois.name: Saan
pois.short: Auckland's best Thai restaurant — northern Thai family recipes, house-made curry pastes and a Ponsonby institution.
pois.commentary: Saan brings authentic northern Thai cooking to Ponsonby Road, with family recipes, house-made curry pastes and a commitment to genuine Thai flavours that sets it apart from the competition. The khao soi (Chiang Mai curry noodle soup) is the signature dish, and the spice levels are properly Thai — they don't dumb it down. The wine and cocktail lists are thoughtfully matched to the food.

reviews.summary: Auckland's most authentic Thai food. The khao soi is legendary. Can be very busy — booking recommended.

pois.name: Muriwai Gannet Colony
pois.short: One of the few mainland gannet colonies in the world — 1,200 pairs nesting on dramatic cliff-top platforms.
pois.commentary: Every August, Australasian gannets arrive at Muriwai to breed, building their numbers to around 1,200 pairs by December. Two viewing platforms put you within metres of the colony. Watch them dive-bomb the surf from 30 metres up — hitting the water at 145 km/h.

reviews.summary: Stunning free attraction. Visitors love the dramatic cliff setting and closeness to the birds. Best September–March.

pois.name: Shakespear Regional Park
pois.short: Predator-free open sanctuary on the Whangaparāoa Peninsula — tīeke, pāteke, and shore birds roam free.
pois.commentary: Shakespear became predator-free in 2011 after a massive community-led pest eradication. Now tīeke (saddleback), pāteke (brown teal), and shore plovers thrive on the headland. Easy walking tracks loop through coastal bush and open farmland with views to Tiritiri Matangi.

reviews.summary: Beautiful free park with native birds everywhere. Great for families. Combine with a Tiritiri Matangi trip.

pois.name: Tāwharanui Open Sanctuary
pois.short: Auckland's most complete open sanctuary — kiwi released here in 2011 now number over 100.
pois.commentary: Tāwharanui is an open sanctuary at the tip of a peninsula north of Auckland. A predator-proof fence across the base keeps the area pest-free. North Island brown kiwi were reintroduced in 2011, tākahē in 2012, and kōkako are being considered. The beach is also one of Auckland's most beautiful.

reviews.summary: A hidden gem. Beach is spectacular and you'll hear kiwi at night. The predator-free bush walks are world-class for birding.

pois.name: Hauraki Gulf Whale & Dolphin Safari
pois.short: Bryde's whales, orca, and common dolphins in the Hauraki Gulf — New Zealand's most accessible whale watching.
pois.commentary: The Hauraki Gulf is home to resident Bryde's whales and is regularly visited by orca pods. Auckland Whale & Dolphin Safari runs research-focused trips where you'll often see common dolphins in pods of hundreds, plus petrels and shearwaters.

reviews.summary: Good chance of sightings year-round. Bryde's whales are the main draw. Orca sightings are seasonal but spectacular.

pois.name: Motuihe Island / Motuhē
pois.short: Predator-free island sanctuary in the Hauraki Gulf — saddleback, kākāriki, and dotterel thrive here.
pois.commentary: Motuihe was declared pest-free in 2005 after a community-led rat and stoat eradication. Saddleback, kākāriki, brown teal, and dotterel have been translocated here. Walking tracks cross the island through regenerating bush and WWII-era ruins.

reviews.summary: Easy day trip from Auckland. Great beach and native birds everywhere. Ferry from downtown.

pois.name: Motutapu Island Restoration
pois.short: New Zealand's largest island pest-eradication project — 1,500 hectares being restored to pre-human ecology.
pois.commentary: Motutapu is connected to Rangitoto by a causeway and is undergoing one of NZ's most ambitious ecological restorations. Predator-free since 2011, the island is seeing the return of species lost for generations. Volunteer planting days are popular.

reviews.summary: More rugged than Tiritiri but rewarding. Combine with Rangitoto climb.

pois.name: Auckland Zoo — NZ Wildlife Precinct
pois.short: Auckland Zoo's NZ precinct features kiwi, tuatara, kea, kākāpō displays, and a native nocturnal house.
pois.commentary: The Te Wao Nui precinct recreates six NZ habitats — coast, islands, wetlands, night, forest, and high country. The nocturnal kiwi house is one of the best in the country, and conservation breeding programmes for kōkako, whio, and Archey's frog operate behind the scenes.

reviews.summary: Excellent native wildlife section. The kiwi viewing is very reliable. Good for rainy days.

pois.name: Long Bay – Ōkura Marine Reserve
pois.short: Protected marine area on Auckland's North Shore — snorkel with snapper, eagle rays, and crayfish.
pois.commentary: Established in 1995, this 980-hectare reserve protects reef systems from Long Bay to Ōkura. Snapper populations have increased tenfold since protection. Snorkelling is best at the rocky reef near Piripiri Point.

reviews.summary: Great snorkelling close to the city. Fish are noticeably bigger and more abundant than unprotected areas.

pois.name: Goat Island Marine Reserve / Te Hāwere-a-Maki
pois.short: NZ's first marine reserve — crystal-clear water teeming with snapper, blue maomao, and crayfish.
pois.commentary: Established in 1975, Goat Island was New Zealand's first marine reserve and proved the concept of marine protection. Snapper here grow to massive sizes, blue maomao school in thousands, and you can snorkel with stingrays in the shallows. Glass-bottom boat tours available for non-swimmers.

reviews.summary: Must-visit for snorkelling. Water clarity is excellent. The fish are incredibly tame and abundant.

pois.name: Ambury Regional Park Shorebirds
pois.short: Manukau Harbour mudflats where thousands of migratory waders — bar-tailed godwit and knot — feed each summer.
pois.commentary: Ambury's tidal flats are one of Auckland's best spots for shorebirds. From September to March, bar-tailed godwits (kuaka) and red knots arrive after their epic non-stop flight from Alaska. Wrybills and NZ dotterels breed nearby.

reviews.summary: Outstanding birding spot close to the city. Best at low tide during summer migration season.

pois.name: Waitākere Ranges — Kōkako Country
pois.short: One of the last mainland strongholds for the North Island kōkako — listen for their haunting organ-like call at dawn.
pois.commentary: The Waitākere Ranges are home to around 100 pairs of North Island kōkako, thanks to decades of intensive pest control. Dawn is the time to hear their extraordinary call. Ark in the Park, a community conservation project in the Cascades, has been a particular success story.

reviews.summary: Magical birding at dawn. The kōkako call is unforgettable. Tracks are well-maintained but kauri dieback protocols apply.

pois.name: Rangitoto Island — Pest-Free Sanctuary
pois.short: Auckland's youngest volcano is now pest-free — little blue penguins and forest birds are returning to the lava fields.
pois.commentary: A massive 2011 aerial poison drop cleared rats and stoats from Rangitoto and neighbouring Motutapu. Little blue penguins now breed along the shore, kākāriki have been released, and the unique pōhutukawa forests are regenerating. The summit walk offers panoramic views.

reviews.summary: Iconic Auckland day trip. The pest-free status means much more birdlife than before. Summit track is a must.

pois.name: Karekare – Ark in the Park Kōkako Trail
pois.short: Community-run conservation area where reintroduced kōkako, hihi, and whitehead are thriving in West Auckland bush.
pois.commentary: Ark in the Park is a 2,270-hectare community pest-control project in the Cascades Kauri area of the Waitākere Ranges. Kōkako were reintroduced in 2006, hihi (stitchbird) in 2007, and whiteheads in 2008. Early morning walks give the best chance of hearing kōkako.

reviews.summary: Real conservation in action. Volunteer days are rewarding. Dawn is best for birding.

pois.name: Wenderholm Regional Park
pois.short: Pūkeko-filled coastal park with mature pōhutukawa forest and NZ dotterel nesting beaches.
pois.commentary: Wenderholm was Auckland's first regional park, established 1965. The pōhutukawa forest is home to kererū, tūī, and fantail, while the beach hosts breeding NZ dotterel. The Schischka homestead area is good for spotting pūkeko and paradise shelduck.

reviews.summary: Beautiful park, great for families. Nice bird variety between the bush and beach habitats.

pois.name: Whatipu Wetland & Caves
pois.short: Wild, remote wetland at the southern head of the Manukau Harbour — bittern, fernbird, and banded rail habitat.
pois.commentary: Whatipu is one of Auckland's wildest spots — a vast sand dune system, wetland, and cave complex at the southern Manukau head. Australasian bittern (one of NZ's rarest birds) breed in the wetlands, and fernbird and banded rail can be heard in the rushes.

reviews.summary: Remote and atmospheric. Not easy to get to but worth it for serious birders. The caves are impressive too.

pois.name: Auckland Art Gallery Toi o Tāmaki
pois.short: New Zealand's largest and most prestigious art gallery, housed in a stunning heritage and modern building.
pois.commentary: Auckland Art Gallery Toi o Tāmaki is New Zealand's largest art institution, holding over 17,000 works spanning seven centuries. The gallery occupies a magnificent French Renaissance-style building from 1887, dramatically extended in 2011 with a canopy of kauri-inspired forms designed by Melbourne architects FJMT and Auckland's Archimedia. The permanent collection includes significant works by Colin McCahon, Frances Hodgkins, and Gottfried Lindauer's famous Māori portraits. Admission to the main collection is free. The gallery hosts major international touring exhibitions throughout the year. Allow at least two hours to explore the permanent galleries, and check the website for current temporary exhibitions. The on-site café offers excellent coffee with views over Albert Park.

reviews.summary: A world-class gallery with an outstanding collection of New Zealand and international art. The building itself is a work of art, blending heritage architecture with contemporary design. Free entry to the permanent collection makes it a must-visit.

pois.name: The Civic Theatre
pois.short: Atmospheric 1929 picture palace with a famous star-lit ceiling, now Auckland's premier performing arts venue.
pois.commentary: The Civic Theatre opened in 1929 as one of the grandest atmospheric theatres in the southern hemisphere. Its auditorium ceiling is designed to resemble a night sky, complete with twinkling stars and projected clouds, creating the illusion of sitting in an exotic open-air courtyard. The lavish Indo-Islamic interior features minarets, domes, panthers, and elephants — an extraordinary fantasy inspired by Indian and Moorish architecture. Seating 2,378 people, it is Auckland's largest theatre and hosts major touring musicals, concerts, and events. The building was nearly demolished in the 1990s but was saved by a passionate public campaign. Heritage tours are occasionally offered, revealing backstage areas and the original Wurlitzer organ. Check the events calendar for current performances.

reviews.summary: An absolutely stunning venue — the starlit ceiling alone is worth the visit. The atmosphere adds magic to any performance. A real piece of Auckland heritage that deserves to be experienced.

pois.name: Q Theatre
pois.short: Auckland's home for bold independent theatre, comedy, and dance in the heart of the city.
pois.commentary: Q Theatre is Auckland's dedicated venue for independent and contemporary performing arts, located on Queen Street near the Aotea Quarter. Opened in 2011, the purpose-built facility contains three performance spaces: the Rangatira (330 seats), the Loft (120 seats), and the Vault, an intimate studio space. The theatre champions new New Zealand works alongside international productions, presenting everything from cutting-edge drama to experimental dance and comedy. The building's striking contemporary design incorporates a dramatic glazed façade. Q Theatre has become essential to Auckland's cultural identity, nurturing emerging artists and hosting festivals including the Auckland Fringe. The on-site bar is a popular pre-show gathering spot. Book online for best seats and check for matinee discounts.

reviews.summary: Great venue for independent theatre with an intimate feel even in the larger spaces. The programming is consistently interesting and adventurous. Bar area is a nice spot before the show.

pois.name: Aotea Centre
pois.short: Auckland's premier multi-purpose performing arts centre hosting concerts, opera, and major cultural events.
pois.commentary: The Aotea Centre is the flagship venue of Auckland's performing arts precinct, opened in 1990 and home to the ASB Theatre (formerly The Herald Theatre) and the Great Hall. It hosts the New Zealand Opera, Auckland Philharmonia Orchestra performances, ballet seasons, and major cultural events. The building anchors the Aotea Quarter arts precinct alongside the Civic Theatre, Aotea Square, and Q Theatre. The Great Hall seats over 2,000 and features excellent acoustics for orchestral performances. Aotea Square outside is a vibrant public gathering place hosting markets, festivals, and outdoor performances throughout the year. The centre underwent significant upgrades as part of the City Rail Link development. Check the venue's calendar for current performances and look for rush tickets on show days.

reviews.summary: A solid performing arts venue with good acoustics and sightlines. The location in the heart of the city makes it very accessible. Aotea Square adds a nice buzz before and after events.

pois.name: Silo Park
pois.short: Vibrant waterfront park with converted cement silos, Friday night markets, and outdoor cinema in summer.
pois.commentary: Silo Park is a creative waterfront space in the Wynyard Quarter, built around the repurposed concrete silos of the former Portland cement works. The park represents Auckland's transformation of its industrial waterfront into a lively public precinct. During summer, Silo Park hosts free outdoor cinema screenings projected onto the silos, creating a unique open-air experience. The popular Friday night markets run weekly with food trucks, craft stalls, and live music. Permanent public artworks dot the landscape, including works by prominent New Zealand artists. The adjacent silos have been creatively adapted — one houses Silo 6, a gallery space for contemporary art installations. The park connects to the wider Wynyard Quarter waterfront walk, playgrounds, and excellent dining options. Best visited on a warm evening for markets or cinema.

reviews.summary: Love the Friday night markets and summer cinema — such a great vibe. The waterfront location is beautiful and there's always something happening. A fantastic example of urban regeneration done right.

pois.name: Te Uru Waitākere Contemporary Gallery
pois.short: Contemporary art gallery nestled in Titirangi's rainforest setting, showcasing innovative New Zealand art.
pois.commentary: Te Uru Waitākere Contemporary Gallery sits at the gateway to the Waitākere Ranges in the artistic village of Titirangi. The striking contemporary building, designed by Mitchell and Stout Architects, was completed in 2014 and features soaring timber ceilings and large windows framing the surrounding native bush. The gallery presents a dynamic programme of contemporary art exhibitions, with a focus on West Auckland artists and experimental practices. Titirangi has been an artists' colony since the 1950s, attracted by the dramatic bush-clad hills and unique light. The gallery hosts regular talks, workshops, and family art activities. Admission is free. Combine your visit with a walk in the Waitākere Ranges or lunch at one of Titirangi's excellent village cafés. The gallery shop features beautiful locally made art and design pieces.

reviews.summary: A gem of a gallery in a beautiful bush setting. The exhibitions are always thought-provoking and the building itself is gorgeous. Worth the drive to Titirangi, especially combined with a bush walk.

pois.name: Ponsonby Road Precinct
pois.short: Auckland's iconic dining and shopping strip, lined with restored Victorian villas and independent boutiques.
pois.commentary: Ponsonby Road is Auckland's most celebrated urban street, stretching from Karangahape Road to Herne Bay through one of the city's most characterful inner suburbs. Once a working-class neighbourhood of colonial villas, Ponsonby transformed from the 1980s into a vibrant precinct of restaurants, cafés, bars, boutiques, and galleries housed in beautifully restored Victorian and Edwardian buildings. The street is home to some of Auckland's finest dining, from upscale restaurants to trendy brunch spots. Independent fashion boutiques sit alongside design stores and specialty food shops. The annual Ponsonby Road street festival draws massive crowds. Side streets reward exploration with hidden cafés and galleries. Start at the Three Lamps intersection and walk northwest, allowing time to duck into side streets. Weekday mornings are quietest; Saturday brunch is a local institution.

reviews.summary: The best street for eating, drinking, and people-watching in Auckland. The restored villas give it real character and charm. Come hungry — there are too many good cafés and restaurants to choose from.

pois.name: Cheltenham Beach
pois.short: Sheltered North Shore beach with stunning views of Rangitoto Island, perfect for swimming and kayaking.
pois.commentary: Cheltenham Beach is one of Auckland's most picturesque harbour beaches, stretching along the North Shore with an uninterrupted view of volcanic Rangitoto Island. The sheltered, north-facing beach offers calm, warm swimming conditions ideal for families, with a gentle sandy bottom that extends well out. The beach is framed by the prestigious Devonport neighbourhood with its charming heritage character. At low tide, the exposed sand flats are perfect for walking and beach cricket. Kayakers and stand-up paddleboarders launch from here to explore the harbour. The iconic Rangitoto vista makes this one of Auckland's most photographed beaches, especially at sunrise. Reach Cheltenham via a pleasant walk from Devonport village, which itself is accessible by a scenic 12-minute ferry ride from downtown Auckland. Limited parking means the ferry option is recommended on summer weekends.

reviews.summary: One of Auckland's most beautiful beaches with that amazing Rangitoto backdrop. The calm water is great for kids and paddleboarding. Take the ferry to Devonport and walk over — it makes a perfect half-day trip.

pois.name: St Heliers Beach
pois.short: Popular eastern suburbs beach with excellent swimming, a vibrant village, and coastal walkway connections.
pois.commentary: St Heliers Beach is a beloved Auckland swimming beach on the inner Hauraki Gulf, backed by a grassy promenade and the charming St Heliers village. The 800-metre sandy beach faces northeast and offers sheltered swimming conditions, making it one of the safer city beaches for families. The beach connects to the Tāmaki Drive coastal walkway, a stunning flat path popular with walkers, runners, and cyclists that stretches all the way to the CBD. The village behind the beach has excellent cafés, restaurants, and an iconic fish-and-chip shop. Vellenoweth Green at the eastern end hosts community events and provides shade for picnics. The beach is patrolled during summer months. Combine a swim with a walk along the coastal path toward Mission Bay for one of Auckland's classic waterfront experiences. Bus connections from the city are frequent.

reviews.summary: A lovely beach with a real village atmosphere. The coastal walk to Mission Bay is one of Auckland's best urban walks. Great cafés nearby for post-swim coffee and brunch.

pois.name: La Cigale French Market
pois.short: Weekend French-inspired artisan market in Parnell with gourmet foods, pastries, and local produce.
pois.commentary: La Cigale French Market operates every Saturday and Sunday morning in the heart of Parnell, Auckland's oldest suburb. Housed in a character warehouse on St Georges Bay Road, the market features around 40 vendors selling French-style pastries, artisan cheeses, charcuterie, fresh breads, organic produce, and specialty coffees. The atmosphere buzzes with live music while you sample crepes, oysters, and handmade chocolates. Arrive before 10am to beat the crowds and secure the best picks. The surrounding Parnell area offers boutique shopping and gallery browsing after your market visit. Free street parking is available on weekends. A must for foodies visiting Auckland.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the authentic French market atmosphere and quality of artisan products. The pastries and cheeses receive particular praise, though some note it can get very crowded by late morning.

pois.name: Hallertau Brewbar & Restaurant
pois.short: Award-winning craft brewery and restaurant set in a rural Riverhead location northwest of Auckland.
pois.commentary: Hallertau is one of New Zealand's pioneering craft breweries, established in 2005 on a rural property in Riverhead, northwest Auckland. Named after the famous hop-growing region in Bavaria, the brewery produces a rotating lineup of beers from crisp pilsners to bold IPAs and seasonal specials. The spacious brewbar and restaurant occupies a converted farmstead with indoor and outdoor seating overlooking green paddocks. The food menu champions local ingredients with wood-fired pizzas and seasonal dishes designed to pair with the beers. Live music events and beer releases draw loyal crowds. The drive from central Auckland takes about 30 minutes and makes for a pleasant afternoon outing through the Kumeu wine district.

reviews.summary: Praised for its relaxed rural setting and consistently excellent craft beers. The wood-fired pizzas and generous portions are highlights. Some visitors note it can be busy on sunny weekends.

pois.name: Sawmill Brewery Matakana
pois.short: Craft brewery taproom in the Matakana wine and food region, an hour north of Auckland city.
pois.commentary: Sawmill Brewery has been crafting beers in the Matakana region since 2004, becoming one of New Zealand's most respected independent breweries. Their taproom offers a full range of core and seasonal beers including their flagship Pilsner and popular Hazy IPA. Located in the heart of the Matakana food and wine trail, the brewery is an easy addition to a day exploring local vineyards, olive groves, and the popular Matakana Village Farmers Market held every Saturday. The taproom features a relaxed indoor-outdoor setting with food trucks or a kitchen serving dishes designed to complement the beers. Combine with visits to nearby Tawharanui Regional Park or Goat Island marine reserve for a full day out of the city.

reviews.summary: Visitors enjoy the quality of the craft beers and the relaxed Matakana vibe. The brewery pairs well with a day exploring the wider region. Staff are knowledgeable and friendly.

pois.name: Auckland Bridge Bungy
pois.short: Bungy jump from Auckland Harbour Bridge, 40 metres above Waitemata Harbour with AJ Hackett.
pois.commentary: AJ Hackett's Auckland Bridge Bungy offers a 40-metre leap from a specially constructed pod attached to the underside of the iconic Auckland Harbour Bridge. Jumpers plunge towards the sparkling waters of the Waitemata Harbour with panoramic views of the city skyline, Devonport, and Rangitoto Island. The experience includes a full safety briefing and professional photos and video packages are available. For those who prefer not to jump, the Bridge Climb option lets you scale the arches of the bridge for spectacular views. The operation runs year-round, weather permitting. Minimum age is 10 years and minimum weight is 35kg. Book online for best rates and arrive 30 minutes before your slot.

reviews.summary: Thrill-seekers rate this as an unforgettable Auckland experience. The harbour views mid-jump are spectacular. Staff are reassuring and professional, making even nervous jumpers feel safe.

pois.name: Auckland Harbour Bridge Climb
pois.short: Guided climb to the summit of Auckland Harbour Bridge for 360-degree views of the city and harbour.
pois.commentary: The Auckland Bridge Climb takes you along catwalks and ladders to the top of the Auckland Harbour Bridge, standing 67 metres above the Waitemata Harbour. Operated by AJ Hackett, the guided experience lasts around 1.5 hours and includes insights into the bridge's construction history, including the clip-on lanes added by Japanese engineers in 1969. From the summit, you enjoy unobstructed 360-degree views spanning the Hauraki Gulf islands, the Sky Tower, volcanic cones, and the distant Waitakere Ranges. All safety equipment is provided, and the climb suits moderate fitness levels. Sunrise and sunset climbs are available for photographers seeking golden light. Departures run from Curran Street in Westhaven.

reviews.summary: A memorable Auckland activity with stunning panoramic views. Guides share fascinating bridge history. Suitable for those who want adventure without the bungy jump intensity.

pois.name: Butterfly Creek
pois.short: Tropical butterfly house and animal park near Auckland Airport featuring exotic species and farm animals.
pois.commentary: Butterfly Creek is a family-friendly attraction located minutes from Auckland Airport, making it an ideal stop before or after a flight. The tropical butterfly house contains hundreds of free-flying butterflies from around the world in a warm, lush environment. Beyond butterflies, the park features a crocodile enclosure, red pandas, meerkats, monkeys, a saltwater aquarium, and a farmyard where children can interact with animals. The Dinosaur Kingdom exhibit appeals to younger visitors with life-size animatronic models. A train ride loops the property. The on-site cafe offers decent food and there are picnic areas throughout the grounds. Allow at least two hours to see everything. Annual passes offer good value for Auckland families.

reviews.summary: Families praise the variety of animals and the butterfly house experience. Convenient airport location is a bonus. Some visitors feel the entry price is slightly high for the size of the park.

pois.name: Manukau Heads Lighthouse
pois.short: Historic 1874 lighthouse at the entrance to Manukau Harbour with views of the treacherous harbour bar.
pois.commentary: Perched on the clifftops at Awhitu Peninsula, Manukau Heads Lighthouse has watched over one of New Zealand's most dangerous harbour entrances since 1874. The harbour bar has claimed numerous vessels, most notably the HMS Orpheus in 1863, New Zealand's worst maritime disaster with 189 lives lost. The lighthouse was relocated from its original position in 1874 to its current site for better visibility. A short walk from the car park leads to the restored lighthouse, which offers commanding views across the Manukau Harbour entrance to the Waitakere coastline. Information panels tell the story of the area's maritime history. The surrounding Awhitu Regional Park features walking tracks through native bush. The drive from Auckland takes about 90 minutes via Waiuku.

reviews.summary: A fascinating historic site with powerful maritime stories and beautiful coastal views. The drive through the Awhitu Peninsula is scenic. The lighthouse itself is well-maintained and informative.

pois.name: Waiheke Island Wine Trail
pois.short: Island wine trail with over 30 vineyards producing acclaimed Bordeaux-style reds, 35 minutes by ferry from Auckland.
pois.commentary: Waiheke Island is Auckland's wine island, home to over 30 vineyards and wineries producing some of New Zealand's finest Bordeaux-style red blends. The island's warm, dry microclimate and clay soils create ideal conditions for Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Syrah, varieties that struggle in cooler New Zealand regions. Leading estates include Stonyridge, whose Larose is one of NZ's most sought-after reds, Mudbrick, Te Motu, Cable Bay, and Man O' War. Many wineries offer cellar door tastings with stunning views across the Hauraki Gulf. The island is accessible by a 35-minute ferry from downtown Auckland, and wine tour options include guided bus tours, self-drive, or cycling between wineries. The combination of world-class wine, beautiful beaches, olive groves, and art galleries makes Waiheke a complete destination experience. Summer weekends are busy, so midweek visits offer a more relaxed pace. Several wineries have excellent restaurants for vineyard dining.

reviews.summary: One of the world's great wine island experiences. The quality of Bordeaux-style reds is remarkable and the island setting is magical. The ferry ride itself sets the tone for a special day out.

pois.name: Matakana Food & Wine Trail
pois.short: Self-drive food and wine trail an hour north of Auckland with vineyards, olive groves, and a famous farmers market.
pois.commentary: The Matakana region, an hour north of Auckland, has developed into one of the upper North Island's most vibrant food and wine destinations. The self-drive trail takes in boutique wineries specialising in warm-climate varieties, olive groves pressing single-estate oils, artisan chocolate makers, local breweries, and numerous cafes championing seasonal local produce. The Matakana Village Farmers Market, held every Saturday morning, is the trail's social heart, bringing together local growers, bakers, and producers in a convivial outdoor setting. Key wine stops include Ascension Wine Estate, Brick Bay Wines with its acclaimed sculpture trail, and Ransom Wines. Morris & James pottery at nearby Matakana is worth visiting for its distinctive glazed ceramics. The region combines rural charm with creative energy, attracting Auckland day-trippers and weekenders year-round. Combine with visits to Tawharanui or Goat Island for a full day.

reviews.summary: A wonderful day out from Auckland with excellent wineries, artisan food, and a fantastic farmers market. The Matakana region has real creative energy. Best visited on a Saturday to catch the market.

pois.name: Wynyard Quarter Waterfront
pois.short: Revitalised waterfront precinct with restaurants, craft bars, fish market, and harbour-edge dining.
pois.commentary: Wynyard Quarter is Auckland's premier waterfront dining and entertainment precinct, transformed from former industrial wharves into a vibrant urban space. The area features a concentration of restaurants, craft beer bars, and cafes along the harbour edge, with North Wharf offering waterside dining with views to the Harbour Bridge. The Auckland Fish Market operates daily with fresh seafood from around New Zealand, and the adjoining Seafood School offers cooking classes. ASB Waterfront Theatre provides performing arts, while public art installations and playgrounds add family appeal. The Silo Park area hosts summer markets and outdoor cinema. The precinct connects to the Viaduct Harbour and is a short walk from Britomart and the CBD. A playground and splash pad area makes it family-friendly by day, while the restaurant and bar scene comes alive in the evenings.

reviews.summary: Auckland's best waterfront dining precinct with excellent variety. The fish market is a highlight and the harbour views are lovely. Gets busy on sunny weekends.

pois.name: Muriwai Beach & Gannet Colony Viewpoint
pois.short: Wild west coast surf beach with spectacular clifftop walks and views over an Australasian gannet colony.
pois.commentary: Muriwai Beach is Auckland's most dramatic west coast beach, a 60km stretch of black iron sand backed by towering cliffs and pounded by Tasman Sea surf. The main attraction beyond the beach itself is the Australasian gannet colony at Otakamiro Point, where around 1,200 breeding pairs nest on cliff ledges and rock stacks from August to March. Viewing platforms provide close-up views of these magnificent seabirds without disturbing them, and interpretive panels explain their biology and migration patterns. The Hilary Trail connects Muriwai to the Waitakere Ranges tramping network. The beach is patrolled for swimming during summer and is popular with surfers year-round. The drive from central Auckland takes about 45 minutes via Kumeu. The gannet colony is one of only three mainland colonies in New Zealand and is free to visit. Sunset at Muriwai is particularly spectacular.

reviews.summary: A stunning west coast beach with the bonus of an incredible gannet colony. The cliff walks are dramatic and the views are vast. One of Auckland's best natural attractions, especially during nesting season.

pois.name: Titirangi Village & Rainforest
pois.short: Bohemian village on Auckland's western fringe surrounded by native rainforest, with galleries and cafes.
pois.commentary: Titirangi is Auckland's village in the bush, a bohemian hillside community perched on the edge of the Waitakere Ranges where suburbia meets native rainforest. The compact village centre features art galleries, boutique shops, excellent cafes, and the historic Lopdell House gallery. The surrounding Waitakere Ranges Regional Park offers hundreds of kilometres of walking tracks through dense native bush, waterfalls, and kauri groves. The Te Uru Waitakere Contemporary Gallery showcases New Zealand art in a striking building with panoramic views. Titirangi has long attracted artists, writers, and creative types, giving it a distinctive character quite different from Auckland's urban core. The village markets operate on the last Sunday of each month. Several bush walks are accessible directly from the village, including tracks leading to cascading streams and swimming holes. A 20-minute drive from the city centre.

reviews.summary: A charming bohemian village with excellent galleries and cafes. The rainforest setting is magical and the bush walks are easily accessible. A refreshing escape from city Auckland.

pois.name: La Cigale French Market
pois.short: European-style artisan food market in Parnell every Saturday and Sunday.
pois.commentary: La Cigale brings a taste of French market culture to Auckland's Parnell neighbourhood every weekend. The covered market features artisan food stalls selling freshly baked croissants and bread, French cheeses, charcuterie, organic produce, and prepared foods from various cuisines. The atmosphere is lively and sociable, with stallholders offering generous tastings. The permanent warehouse space also houses food retailers open during the week. Saturday mornings are busiest, with locals gathering for coffee and pastries in the courtyard. The market has been running since 2008 and has become a beloved Auckland food institution. Arrive early on Saturdays for the best selection and a more relaxed experience before the crowds build.

reviews.summary: A wonderful food market with an authentic European feel. The pastries and cheeses are outstanding. Saturday mornings are the best time to visit.

pois.name: Auckland Art Gallery Toi o Tāmaki
pois.short: NZ's largest art institution — over 17,000 works spanning 700 years in a stunning heritage building with contemporary extension.
pois.commentary: Auckland Art Gallery Toi o Tāmaki houses more than 17,000 works spanning seven centuries, making it New Zealand's most comprehensive visual arts collection. The original 1887 French Renaissance building was joined by a dramatic kauri-and-glass extension in 2011, winning a World Architecture Festival award. The Māori and Pacific collection is exceptional, with works by Gottfried Lindauer, C.F. Goldie, and contemporary artists like Lisa Reihana. Entry to the main collection is free — one of Auckland's finest cultural gifts.

reviews.summary: Visitors praise the breadth of the permanent collection and the striking architecture. The Māori portraiture rooms are consistently singled out as highlights. Free entry makes it easy to visit repeatedly.

pois.name: MOTAT — Museum of Transport and Technology
pois.short: Two sites of vintage aircraft, trams, and technology — the Lancaster bomber and Solent flying boat are highlights.
pois.commentary: MOTAT sprawls across two sites in Western Springs, connected by a working vintage tram. The aviation collection is the centrepiece: a rare Avro Lancaster bomber, a Short Solent flying boat, and a de Havilland Mosquito among dozens of aircraft. Beyond aviation, there are steam engines, early telecommunications gear, printing presses, and hands-on science exhibits for kids. It's a genuinely engaging museum that covers New Zealand's technological history with surprising depth.

reviews.summary: Families and aviation enthusiasts rate MOTAT highly for its interactive exhibits and impressive aircraft collection. The vintage tram ride between sites is a favourite. Some note the two-site layout requires extra time.

pois.name: Stardome Observatory & Planetarium
pois.short: Planetarium shows and telescope viewing nights in One Tree Hill Domain — Auckland's window to the southern sky.
pois.commentary: Stardome sits in the shadow of Maungakiekie / One Tree Hill, offering planetarium shows, interactive exhibits, and regular telescope viewing nights. The full-dome digital theatre projects the southern sky with stunning clarity, and the shows cover everything from Māori star lore to deep space. Friday and Saturday night telescope sessions let you see Saturn's rings and Jupiter's moons for yourself. A perfect rainy-day or evening activity that most tourists miss.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the planetarium shows and telescope nights — especially for families. The Māori astronomy content is praised as unique. Some wish the exhibit space were larger.

pois.name: Wenderholm Regional Park
pois.short: Pōhutukawa-fringed beach and bush walks at Auckland's oldest regional park — a stunning estuary escape north of the city.
pois.commentary: Wenderholm was Auckland's first regional park, established in 1965 to protect a magnificent stand of pōhutukawa trees along the Pūhoi River estuary. The beach is sheltered and safe for swimming, with views across to Mahurangi. Walking tracks loop through coastal bush and farmland, with the Schischka Track climbing to panoramic views. At Christmas, the pōhutukawa canopy turns crimson — one of Auckland's most beautiful seasonal sights. Pack a picnic and allow a lazy half-day.

reviews.summary: Visitors love the combination of safe swimming beach, bush walks, and pōhutukawa-lined estuary. A favourite picnic spot for Auckland families. The drive north from the city is scenic in itself.

pois.name: Piha Beach & Lion Rock
pois.short: Auckland's most iconic surf beach — dramatic black sand, pounding waves, and the sentinel of Lion Rock.
pois.commentary: Piha is Auckland's wild side — a dramatic west coast surf beach backed by bush-clad ranges and dominated by the 101-metre Lion Rock, a volcanic remnant that splits the beach into north and south. The black iron-sand absorbs heat and the Tasman swells are powerful, so this is a serious surf beach with strong rip currents. Swim between the flags. The Kitekite Falls track (45 minutes return) starts at the south end and is one of Auckland's best short bush walks. Piha has been the backdrop to countless NZ films and the TV series Piha Rescue.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Piha as raw, dramatic, and unmissable. Lion Rock is universally praised as iconic. Strong warnings about rip currents — swim only between the flags.

pois.name: Goat Island Marine Reserve
pois.short: NZ's oldest marine reserve — crystal-clear water teeming with fish just an hour north of Auckland.
pois.commentary: Established in 1975, Goat Island / Te Hāwere-a-Maki was New Zealand's first marine reserve, and the results of five decades of protection are spectacular. Snorkellers are greeted by blue maomao, snapper, and crayfish within metres of the shore. The water clarity on a good day rivals tropical reefs. Glass-bottom boat tours run for non-swimmers. The Leigh marine lab next door runs educational programmes. Visit on a weekday in summer for the best experience — weekends can be crowded.

reviews.summary: Snorkellers consistently rave about the abundance and tameness of fish. The clarity of the water is a highlight. Parking can be challenging on summer weekends.

pois.name: Tiritiri Matangi Island
pois.short: Open wildlife sanctuary in the Hauraki Gulf — fearless native birds including takahē, kōkako, and hihi.
pois.commentary: Tiritiri Matangi is one of New Zealand's greatest conservation success stories. Once stripped bare by farming, the island has been replanted by thousands of volunteers since 1984 and declared predator-free. Today it's an open sanctuary where endangered species thrive: takahē waddle across the paths, kōkako sing from the canopy, and hihi (stitchbird) feed at sugar-water stations. The ferry runs from Auckland's Gulf Harbour, and volunteer guides lead walks that bring the conservation story to life. Book ahead — numbers are limited to protect the island.

reviews.summary: Visitors describe Tiritiri Matangi as a life-changing wildlife experience. Seeing takahē and kōkako up close is rated unforgettable. The volunteer-led conservation story adds real depth.

pois.name: Karekare Beach
pois.short: Hauntingly beautiful black sand beach made famous by The Piano — backed by bush and a waterfall.
pois.commentary: Karekare is wilder and lonelier than its neighbour Piha — a long sweep of black sand beneath the Waitākere Ranges that feels genuinely remote despite being only 40 minutes from the CBD. Jane Campion filmed the iconic beach scenes of The Piano here in 1992, and the atmosphere hasn't changed. Karekare Falls drops 30 metres into a pool just a short walk from the car park. The beach is unpatrolled and dangerous for swimming — come for the drama, the walks, and the solitude rather than the surf.

reviews.summary: Visitors praise Karekare as Auckland's most atmospheric beach. The Piano connection adds cultural cachet. Strong warnings about swimming — this is for walking and photography, not swimming.

pois.name: Hunua Ranges Regional Park
pois.short: Auckland's wildest park — Hunua Falls, extensive bush walks, swimming holes, and mountain biking trails.
pois.commentary: The Hunua Ranges are Auckland's largest and wildest regional park — 16,000 hectares of dense native bush southeast of the city. Hunua Falls is the showpiece: a powerful 30-metre cascade into a deep swimming hole, reached by a short walk from the road. Beyond the falls, the park offers multi-day tramping tracks, mountain biking trails, and the Cossey and Mangatangi dams that supply much of Auckland's water. Kōkako have been reintroduced to the ranges, and you might hear their haunting organ-like call on an early morning walk.

reviews.summary: Bush walkers and mountain bikers praise the Hunua Ranges as Auckland's best backcountry escape. Hunua Falls is the standout attraction. Some tracks are muddy after rain — bring proper footwear.

pois.name: Ponsonby Road Food & Cafe Scene
pois.short: Auckland's most celebrated dining strip — diverse cuisine, excellent coffee, and vibrant street life.
pois.commentary: Ponsonby Road is Auckland's culinary main street — a 2km strip of cafes, restaurants, and bars that runs from Karangahape Road to Herne Bay. The range is extraordinary: from Sidart's progressive Indian-NZ degustation to Prego's Italian classics, from Blue Breeze Inn's modern Chinese to Orphans Kitchen's paddock-to-plate ethos. The brunch scene is legendary — Dizengoff and Kokako are institutions. On a Saturday morning, grab a flat white from one of the specialty coffee spots and watch Auckland at its most cosmopolitan.

reviews.summary: Foodies rate Ponsonby Road as Auckland's essential dining destination. The diversity of cuisines in such a short strip is praised. Weekday visits avoid the weekend brunch queues.

pois.name: Tiritiri Matangi Open Sanctuary
pois.short: An open wildlife sanctuary in the Hauraki Gulf accessible by ferry from Auckland, home to some of New Zealand's most endangered native birds.
pois.commentary: Tiritiri Matangi is a 220-hectare island sanctuary managed by DOC, where decades of pest eradication and native planting have created a haven for species like takahe, little spotted kiwi, brown teal, and saddleback. The island is reached by a 75-minute ferry from Auckland's downtown terminal, making it an accessible day trip that feels genuinely remote. Guided walks with knowledgeable volunteers reveal the conservation story behind the island's transformation from farmland to thriving ecosystem. Birdwatching here is extraordinary — species that are functionally extinct on the mainland wander past within metres. The island lighthouse, rebuilt in 1864, adds a heritage dimension to the visit.

reviews.summary: Visitors consistently describe Tiritiri Matangi as a highlight of their New Zealand trip, with the density and tameness of rare native birds being genuinely astonishing. The volunteer guides are praised for their passion and depth of knowledge.

pois.name: Shakespear Regional Park Open Sanctuary
pois.short: A fenced open sanctuary at the tip of the Whangaparaoa Peninsula offering coastal scenery and encounters with native birds including pied stilts and kereru.
pois.commentary: Shakespear Regional Park sits at the northernmost tip of the Whangaparaoa Peninsula and is enclosed by a predator-proof fence that protects a growing population of native wildlife. Pohutukawa-lined beaches frame spectacular views across the Hauraki Gulf toward Tiritiri Matangi and the volcanic silhouette of Rangitoto. Walking tracks traverse headlands and sheltered bays, passing paddocks where pukeko and paradise shelducks forage alongside domestic farm animals kept as part of the park's character. The park is less visited than central Auckland's parks, giving it a quiet, unhurried atmosphere ideal for birdwatching and picnicking.

reviews.summary: A beloved local escape praised for its combination of beach access, native bush, and wildlife, with the predator-free sanctuary adding genuine conservation value. Families and serious walkers alike find something to love here.

pois.name: New Zealand Maritime Museum
pois.short: Auckland's foremost maritime museum on the Viaduct Harbour, tracing New Zealand's deep sea-going heritage from Polynesian waka to modern racing yachts.
pois.commentary: Situated at the heart of Auckland's Viaduct Harbour, the New Zealand Maritime Museum holds the country's most extensive collection of naval and maritime artefacts. Its galleries cover Polynesian wayfinding and the double-hulled waka hourua that first brought Maori ancestors to Aotearoa, through to European exploration, the nation's fishing and trading history, and New Zealand's celebrated America's Cup campaigns. Visitors can board restored vessels moored alongside the museum and take harbour cruises on heritage craft. The museum's location means the ambient backdrop of modern superyachts and working ferries perfectly reinforces the stories being told inside.

reviews.summary: The museum earns strong marks for its breadth and the quality of its Polynesian voyaging exhibits, which visitors find genuinely moving and informative. The option to board heritage vessels is a popular highlight.

pois.name: Stardome Observatory and Planetarium
pois.short: Auckland's only professional observatory and planetarium, nestled within the slopes of Maungakiekie One Tree Hill, offering star shows and telescope viewing nights.
pois.commentary: Te Whatu Stardome sits at 670 Manukau Road within the Maungakiekie One Tree Hill Domain, combining a public planetarium with a working observatory that houses several telescopes including a 40-centimetre Meade telescope. Evening shows project New Zealand's southern sky in vivid detail across the dome ceiling, while telescope viewing nights on clear evenings allow visitors to observe the moon, planets, and deep-sky objects with expert guidance. The location on a volcanic cone adds an extra layer of Auckland geology to the experience, and the surrounding domain provides a pleasant approach walk through native trees and farmland grazed by sheep.

reviews.summary: Families praise the accessible and engaging planetarium shows, while astronomy enthusiasts appreciate the rare opportunity for guided telescope viewing in an urban setting. Clear nights attract long queues at the eyepiece.

pois.name: Maungauika / North Head Historic Reserve
pois.short: A volcanic headland in Devonport riddled with military tunnels and gun emplacements, offering sweeping views across the Waitemata Harbour entrance.
pois.commentary: Maungauika forms the eastern sentinel at the entrance to Auckland's Waitemata Harbour and carries layers of history from Maori settlement through to Cold War-era military fortification. The headland contains an extraordinary network of tunnels and bunkers carved into the volcanic scoria during the 1880s and expanded through both World Wars, some of which are accessible on self-guided walks. As one of Auckland's 53 tūpuna maunga, the site is co-managed by the Tūpuna Maunga Authority and Auckland Council, and its Maori heritage predates the European defences by centuries. Views from the summit take in the inner Hauraki Gulf, Rangitoto Island, the central business district skyline, and the bridge.

reviews.summary: The combination of volcanic geology, Maori history, and military heritage in a single easily walked reserve makes North Head one of Auckland's most rewarding short excursions. The free ferry ride from downtown Auckland to Devonport is part of the appeal.

pois.name: Silo Park Wynyard Quarter
pois.short: A waterfront public space built around repurposed industrial silos in Wynyard Quarter, hosting outdoor cinema, markets, and creative events year-round.
pois.commentary: Silo Park on Jellicoe Street represents Auckland's most successful industrial heritage conversion, transforming grain silos and wharfside infrastructure into a beloved civic gathering space at the western edge of Wynyard Quarter. In summer, the park hosts Friday night outdoor cinema screenings that draw large crowds spreading picnic blankets across the grass under the towering concrete silos. Weekend markets, children's water play areas, and a collection of rotating food trucks make it a destination in its own right rather than simply a transit space. The park anchors the creative and cultural identity of Auckland's waterfront regeneration and sits within walking distance of the Maritime Museum and Viaduct Harbour.

reviews.summary: Locals and visitors rate Silo Park highly as a relaxed waterfront gathering place with genuine character from its industrial heritage. The summer outdoor cinema events are mentioned repeatedly as a quintessential Auckland experience.

pois.name: Rangitoto Island Summit Walk
pois.short: A ferry trip to New Zealand's youngest and largest volcano, with a summit track through lava fields and pohutukawa forest to panoramic views of Auckland.
pois.commentary: Rangitoto erupted around 600 years ago, making it the most recently formed of Auckland's 53 volcanoes, and its symmetrical cone rising 260 metres from the Hauraki Gulf is the defining feature of the Auckland skyline. Ferries depart Auckland's Quay Street terminal and take 25 minutes to the wharf on the island's northwestern shore. The summit track crosses vast black lava fields colonised by thousands of pohutukawa trees — the largest pohutukawa forest in the world — with detours to lava caves near the summit. The 360-degree view from the top takes in the city, the outer islands, the Coromandel Peninsula, and on clear days, the Northland coast.

reviews.summary: The combination of an affordable ferry ride, unique volcanic geology, and one of Auckland's finest viewpoints makes Rangitoto consistently one of the region's top-rated experiences. The lava cave detour is highly recommended.

pois.name: Waipu Cove Surf and Wildlife Walk
pois.short: A scenic coastal walk near Waipu on the Northland/Auckland border where little blue penguins nest in sand dunes and Australasian gannets soar over the surf.
pois.commentary: Waipu Cove sits at the southern end of the Bream Bay coastline north of Auckland and is a quiet surf beach known among naturalists for little blue penguin burrows in the sand dunes behind the beach, most active from August to January. The surrounding coastline is one of the most ecologically diverse in the upper North Island, with the combination of open ocean, rocky reef, and estuarine habitat supporting a wide variety of wading birds, shorebirds, and seabirds. The walk north from the cove along the coast passes colonies of breeding variable oystercatchers and connects to the Waipu River estuary wetlands, an important migratory shorebird stopover. The nearby town of Waipu celebrates its unique Scottish Highland heritage with a famous New Year Highland Games.

reviews.summary: A pleasantly low-key wildlife and coastal walking destination within two hours of Auckland. The combination of surf beach, penguin habitat, and abundant shorebirds makes it a rewarding stopover on the Northland drive.

pois.name: Motutapu Island Restoration Walk
pois.short: A working island restoration project in the Hauraki Gulf connected to Rangitoto Island by a causeway, combining WWII heritage with large-scale native revegetation.
pois.commentary: Motutapu Island lies between Rangitoto and Waiheke in the Hauraki Gulf and is the site of one of New Zealand's largest community-led native forest restoration projects, with thousands of volunteers having planted more than 500,000 native plants since 1994. The island was farmed for most of the 20th century and retains an extensive network of WWII military infrastructure including concrete gun emplacements, observation posts, and underground ammunition stores from the island's role as a harbour defence installation. A causeway connects Motutapu to neighbouring Rangitoto, allowing walkers to combine the two islands' very different landscapes in a single day. North Island saddleback, robin, and whitehead have been reintroduced to the island as the restored forest matures.

reviews.summary: Motutapu offers a rewarding combination of conservation story, WWII heritage, and open island landscapes for those willing to venture beyond the more famous neighbouring islands. The Rangitoto-Motutapu loop is a satisfying full-day walk.

pois.name: Stonyridge Vineyard
pois.short: Iconic Waiheke Island winery producing world-class Bordeaux-style reds.
pois.commentary: Stonyridge Vineyard on Waiheke Island produces Larose, consistently rated among New Zealand's finest red wines and a benchmark Bordeaux-style blend. The vineyard occupies a sun-drenched north-facing hillside with views across the Hauraki Gulf. The cellar door offers tastings, and the on-site cafe Veranda serves Mediterranean-inspired cuisine paired with the estate wines. The annual Stonyridge Festival in February is one of Waiheke's premier events. Visiting requires a ferry from Auckland's CBD to Waiheke (35 minutes), then a short bus or bike ride.

reviews.summary: World-class wines in a stunning island setting. Larose is extraordinary. The Veranda cafe lunch paired with wines is a perfect afternoon.

pois.name: Rangitoto Island
pois.short: Auckland's iconic volcanic island with summit track through the world's largest pohutukawa forest.
pois.commentary: Rangitoto Island is Auckland's most iconic landmark, a symmetrical volcanic cone that erupted from the Hauraki Gulf just 600 years ago. The summit track climbs through the world's largest pohutukawa forest, growing directly on rough lava flows, to a 360-degree panorama taking in the entire Auckland region. The island is predator-free and home to shore birds, forest birds, and coastal invertebrates. A side track leads to lava caves that can be explored with a torch. Fullers ferries depart from downtown Auckland and the journey takes 25 minutes.

reviews.summary: An easy, accessible adventure from downtown Auckland. Summit views are spectacular. The lava caves add excitement. Bring water and sun protection.

pois.name: Western Springs / Wai Orea
pois.short: Urban lakeside reserve with abundant birdlife and peaceful walking tracks.
pois.commentary: Western Springs is a natural spring-fed lake surrounded by mature trees and green space, providing a surprising pocket of wilderness minutes from central Auckland. The lake supports large populations of black swans, pukeko, shags, and eels, with a boardwalk offering close-up viewing. The park connects to Auckland Zoo and MOTAT via walking paths, making it easy to combine visits. The springs have cultural significance as a traditional Maori food-gathering site. On calm mornings the lake reflects the surrounding trees beautifully. Free to visit and popular with joggers, families, and birdwatchers.

reviews.summary: A peaceful green oasis in the city. Excellent birdwatching and pleasant walking. Easy to combine with Zoo or MOTAT visits.

pois.name: Onehunga Bay Reserve / Foreshore
pois.short: Restored harbour foreshore with lagoon swimming, playgrounds, and Mangere Mountain views.
pois.commentary: Onehunga Bay Reserve is a well-kept secret among Auckland locals — a restored foreshore on the Manukau Harbour with a safe tidal lagoon for swimming, extensive playgrounds, and views across to Mangere Mountain. The reserve was transformed from an industrial waterfront into a community recreation space and includes barbecue areas, walking paths, and a cafe. The lagoon provides sheltered swimming that's much calmer than the west coast beaches. The volcanic cones of Mangere Mountain, Maungakiekie/One Tree Hill, and Rarotonga/Mount Smart provide a dramatic backdrop. It's particularly popular with families on summer weekends.

reviews.summary: A great family-friendly spot that most tourists miss. The lagoon is safe for kids and the playground is excellent.

pois.name: Matakana Village & Market
pois.short: Charming village with Saturday farmers market, boutique wineries, and artisan food.
pois.commentary: Matakana Village, an hour north of Auckland, has evolved from a quiet rural settlement into a thriving artisan food and wine destination. The Saturday Matakana Village Farmers Market draws crowds for local produce, artisan bread, cheeses, and street food. The surrounding area has over a dozen boutique wineries producing excellent Pinot Gris, Chardonnay, and Syrah. The Matakana Cinema, a small independent movie house, adds quirky charm. Nearby attractions include Tawharanui Regional Park, Morris & James Pottery, and Sculptureum art park. The village is compact and walkable, with cafes, galleries, and a chocolate factory within strolling distance.

reviews.summary: The Saturday market is outstanding. Excellent wineries nearby. The village has genuine charm without feeling contrived.

pois.name: Maungauika / North Head
pois.short: Historic military fortification with tunnels and panoramic harbour views.
pois.commentary: Maungauika / North Head is a volcanic cone at the entrance to the Waitemata Harbour, fortified in the 1880s against feared Russian invasion and maintained as a military installation through both world wars. Today visitors can explore a network of tunnels and gun emplacements, with interpretive panels explaining the fort's military history. The summit provides one of Auckland's finest panoramic views, taking in the harbour, Rangitoto Island, the city skyline, and the Hauraki Gulf. The short walk from Devonport village takes about 15 minutes. Combined with nearby Takarunga/Mount Victoria, it makes for an excellent half-day outing accessible by ferry from the CBD.

reviews.summary: Fascinating tunnels and gun emplacements with incredible harbour views. Easy to reach by ferry to Devonport.

pois.name: Sculptureum
pois.short: Expansive sculpture park with 10 themed gardens and over 500 artworks.
pois.commentary: Sculptureum in the Matakana wine region is an ambitious private art collection set across 10 themed gardens covering diverse artistic traditions from African and Asian art to contemporary New Zealand sculpture. Over 500 works are displayed in thoughtfully designed garden settings, creating an immersive art experience unlike any other in the country. Three gallery buildings house additional collections including Murano glass and European decorative arts. The on-site Rothko restaurant serves fine dining with vineyard views. The scale and quality of the collection is genuinely surprising for a private venture in rural Auckland. Allow at least two hours to explore the gardens properly.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary private collection in a beautiful setting. The gardens are thoughtfully designed. Rothko restaurant is excellent.

pois.name: Te Henga / Bethells Beach
pois.short: Wild black-sand beach with towering dunes, freshwater lake, and bush walks.
pois.commentary: Te Henga (Bethells Beach) is one of Auckland's most dramatic west coast beaches — a kilometre of black iron sand backed by towering sand dunes and accessed through a valley of bush-clad hills. Lake Wainamu, a freshwater lake hidden behind the dunes, is reached by a 45-minute walk through bush and across sand dunes and is a popular summer swimming spot. The beach is wilder and less crowded than nearby Piha, attracting those who prefer a more remote experience. Body-surfing is excellent when conditions are right but the beach is unpatrolled so exercise caution. The surrounding Waitakere Ranges provide extensive hiking options. The horse riding trails along the beach are popular.

reviews.summary: Auckland's wildest accessible beach. Lake Wainamu is a hidden gem. Less crowded than Piha with equally dramatic scenery.

pois.name: Waiheke Island Wine Trail
pois.short: Award-winning wineries on a beautiful Hauraki Gulf island — 40 minutes by ferry from downtown Auckland.
pois.commentary: Waiheke Island, just 40 minutes by ferry from Auckland's CBD, is home to over 30 wineries producing exceptional wines in a microclimate warmer and drier than the mainland. The island's boutique wineries — many with stunning views across the Hauraki Gulf — produce outstanding Bordeaux-style reds, Syrah, and Chardonnay. The compact geography makes wine-touring easy, whether by rental bike, hop-on-hop-off bus, or guided tour. Highlights include Mudbrick, Cable Bay, and Stonyridge. Many wineries have excellent restaurants, and the island's olive oil producers add another tasting dimension. The combination of world-class wine, beautiful beaches, and the ferry journey makes Waiheke one of Auckland's premier experiences.

reviews.summary: World-class wines in a stunning island setting. The ferry journey sets the scene. Excellent restaurants at the wineries.

pois.name: Ponsonby Road Dining Precinct
pois.short: Auckland's premier dining strip — diverse restaurants, bars, and cafes in a vibrant inner-city neighbourhood.
pois.commentary: Ponsonby Road is Auckland's most celebrated dining and entertainment strip — a kilometre-long stretch of diverse restaurants, cocktail bars, and cafes in one of the city's most vibrant inner-city neighbourhoods. The dining options span cultures and budgets, from upscale Pacific Rim dining to Malaysian street food, Italian trattorias, and Japanese izakaya. The cocktail bar scene is excellent, and the weekend brunch culture is legendary. Beyond food, Ponsonby Road features boutique shopping, galleries, and a creative, slightly bohemian character that distinguishes it from Auckland's corporate CBD. The street comes alive in the evenings and on weekend mornings.
