reviews.summary: Auckland's best dining street with outstanding variety. The weekend brunch scene is unmissable. Good cocktail bars too.

pois.name: Matakana Village Farmers' Market
pois.short: Award-winning Saturday morning farmers' market in a charming wine village north of Auckland.
pois.commentary: The Matakana Village Farmers' Market is one of Auckland's premier weekend food destinations — a Saturday-morning gathering of artisan food producers, organic growers, and specialty vendors in the charming wine village of Matakana. The market is set around the village's distinctive cinema and boutique shops. Local producers sell organic vegetables, artisan breads, award-winning cheeses, olive oil, free-range eggs, and prepared foods. The surrounding Matakana wine region offers cellar doors within easy driving distance. The village atmosphere is relaxed and upscale — a world away from suburban Auckland despite being only 50 minutes north. The market runs year-round, rain or shine.

reviews.summary: Outstanding quality market in a lovely village setting. The artisan food is excellent. Combine with wine tasting nearby.

pois.name: Piha Beach & Lion Rock
pois.short: Auckland's iconic west coast surf beach dominated by the dramatic Lion Rock formation.
pois.commentary: Piha is Auckland's most famous beach — a dramatic stretch of black iron sand on the wild west coast, dominated by the towering Lion Rock formation that splits the beach in two. The surf here is powerful and consistent, making Piha one of New Zealand's premier surf spots (but dangerous for inexperienced swimmers — the rips are serious). Lion Rock can be climbed at low tide for panoramic views along the coast. The Kitekite Falls walk (45 minutes return) leads to a beautiful three-tiered waterfall in the bush behind the beach. Piha has a strong surf culture and creative community. The drive from Auckland through the Waitakere Ranges rainforest is scenic. Piha Surf Rescue is one of NZ's busiest — respect the ocean.

reviews.summary: Dramatic, wild, and beautiful. The surf is powerful — swim between the flags only. Lion Rock is iconic. Kitekite Falls is a lovely walk.

pois.name: Devonport Historic Village
pois.short: Historic naval village with heritage buildings, volcanic summits, and harbour ferry from downtown Auckland.
pois.commentary: Devonport is a charming historic village on Auckland's North Shore, accessible by a scenic 12-minute ferry from downtown. The village retains its Victorian and Edwardian character with heritage buildings, boutique shopping, and excellent cafes. The twin volcanic summits of Mount Victoria and North Head provide panoramic views of Auckland's skyline, the Hauraki Gulf, and Rangitoto Island. North Head is honeycombed with WWII-era tunnels and gun emplacements that can be explored. The naval base gives Devonport a distinctive character. The ferry ride itself provides excellent views of the harbour bridge and city skyline. Devonport offers a complete half-day experience — ferry, walking, history, dining, and views.

reviews.summary: A perfect Auckland half-day trip. The ferry ride, volcanic summits, and village charm combine beautifully. WWII tunnels are fascinating.

pois.name: Te Henga Walkway / Bethells Beach Track
pois.short: Coastal walk through wetlands and dunes to a wild west coast beach.
pois.commentary: The Te Henga Walkway is a stunning coastal walk connecting Bethells Beach to the Goldie Bush walkway, traversing sand dunes, wetlands, and coastal bush along Auckland's dramatic west coast. The full walkway is about 7km one way, passing through the Te Henga Wetland — one of the last remaining dune lakes and wetlands on Auckland's west coast. The black-sand beach at Bethells is wild and beautiful, flanked by towering sand dunes that can be climbed for panoramic views. The area is popular for horse riding, with stables offering beach rides. Lake Wainamu, a freshwater dune lake accessible via a sandy walk, is a hidden swimming gem. The walkway forms part of the Hillary Trail network through the Waitākere Ranges.

reviews.summary: The coastal scenery is stunning and the dune lakes are a hidden gem. The walk is varied and interesting. A beautiful escape from Auckland.

pois.name: Karangahape Road / K Road
pois.short: Auckland's bohemian creative strip with galleries, bars, and diverse dining.
pois.commentary: Karangahape Road (K Road) is Auckland's most culturally vibrant street, a bohemian strip running along a ridge above the city centre. The road has been a hub of counterculture, creativity, and diversity for over a century, and today hosts a thriving arts scene with dozens of galleries, independent shops, live music venues, and some of Auckland's best restaurants and bars. The K Road art mile features commercial and artist-run galleries showing cutting-edge contemporary art. Dining ranges from late-night ramen joints to fine dining, with strong Pacific Island and Asian culinary influences. The street comes alive at night with live music and bustling eateries. The area's character has survived the construction of the new City Rail Link station beneath it.

reviews.summary: Culture lovers appreciate K Road's authentic, diverse character. The gallery scene is impressive, the food is excellent, and the nightlife is Auckland's best.

pois.name: Stony Batter Historic Reserve / Waiheke Island
pois.short: Secret WWII tunnels and gun emplacements on Waiheke Island's eastern tip.
pois.commentary: Stony Batter Historic Reserve on the eastern end of Waiheke Island preserves a network of WWII tunnels and gun emplacements built in 1942 to defend the Hauraki Gulf from potential Japanese invasion. The tunnels extend over 1.5km underground, connecting ammunition magazines, barracks, and two massive 9.2-inch gun emplacements that were never fired in anger. Self-guided tours with torches explore the atmospheric tunnels, while the surrounding reserve features walking tracks through regenerating bush with views across the Hauraki Gulf to the Coromandel Peninsula. The reserve is managed by DOC and is about a 25-minute drive from Waiheke's ferry terminal. Bring a torch and warm clothing for the tunnels. A fascinating piece of NZ's wartime history.

reviews.summary: History enthusiasts love exploring the tunnels. The WWII story is fascinating and the views from the reserve are stunning. Bring a good torch. A different side of Waiheke.

pois.name: Tiritiri Matangi Island Bird Sanctuary
pois.short: One of the world's most successful open conservation projects — a predator-free island alive with rare native birds, a ferry ride from Auckland.
pois.commentary: Tiritiri Matangi Island is a conservation triumph. Once bare farmland, the 220-hectare island in the Hauraki Gulf has been replanted with native bush and declared predator-free, creating a haven for some of New Zealand's rarest birds. Walking the island's tracks, you'll encounter takahē, kōkako, saddleback, stitchbird and North Island robin at close range — species that have vanished from most of the mainland. The kōkako's haunting song alone is worth the ferry trip. Regular ferries run from Auckland's Gulf Harbour. Guided walks with volunteer guides dramatically enhance the experience and are highly recommended.

reviews.summary: An extraordinary wildlife experience. The birds are abundant and approachable. Volunteer guides are excellent. The ferry ride is scenic. Book ahead in summer. Bring lunch and water — no shop on the island.

pois.name: Waiheke Island Food & Wine Trail
pois.short: A day of cellar-door wine tasting, olive oil sampling and vineyard dining on Waiheke Island — Auckland's Mediterranean escape.
pois.commentary: Waiheke Island is a 40-minute ferry from Auckland but feels like a different country. Over 30 boutique wineries produce excellent Bordeaux-style reds, along with olive groves, artisan food producers and several world-class restaurants with vineyard views. A self-drive or guided tour connecting cellar doors — Stonyridge, Cable Bay, Man O' War, Te Motu, Mudbrick — is one of Auckland's finest day-out options. Between tastings, swim at one of the island's golden-sand beaches. The combination of wine, food, beaches and a 40-minute commute from central Auckland makes Waiheke one of the world's great wine islands.

reviews.summary: A world-class food and wine experience. The wineries are excellent. The island setting is stunning. The ferry ride adds to the journey. Book restaurants ahead in summer. Guided tours handle transport well.

pois.name: Wynyard Quarter & Silo Park
pois.short: Auckland's revitalised waterfront precinct — restaurants, markets, playgrounds and harbour views on former industrial wharves.
pois.commentary: Wynyard Quarter is Auckland's most successful urban regeneration project, transforming former working wharves into a vibrant waterfront precinct. The area now hosts restaurants, bars, the Auckland Fish Market, ASB Waterfront Theatre, and Silo Park — an outdoor events space built around converted industrial silos that hosts night markets, outdoor cinema and live music. The playground is one of the best in the city, and the promenade offers excellent harbour walking with views to the Harbour Bridge and Rangitoto. Weekend mornings see a bustling farmer's market, and the evening dining scene rivals anywhere in Auckland.

reviews.summary: A vibrant waterfront area with excellent dining. The Fish Market is a highlight. Silo Park events are fun. The playground is great for families. Parking can be challenging — consider walking or using the ferry.

pois.name: Great Barrier Island / Aotea Nature Experience
pois.short: Auckland's wild frontier — a roadless, off-grid island with hot springs, pristine bush, dark skies, and a self-sufficient community.
pois.commentary: Great Barrier Island (Aotea) is just 90 km from Auckland but feels like another country. The island has no mains electricity, no street lights, and no reticulated water — the community is entirely self-sufficient. This makes it one of the best dark sky sites in the North Island. The bush-clad interior shelters excellent walking tracks, including the summit of Mount Hobson (Hirakimata) through ancient kauri forest. The natural hot springs at Kaitoke are a highlight — soaking in naturally heated pools surrounded by bush. Beaches are wild and beautiful, the fishing is excellent, and the pace of life is refreshingly slow. Access is by ferry (4.5 hours) or small plane (30 minutes).

reviews.summary: A genuinely wild island experience close to Auckland. The hot springs are wonderful. The dark skies are stunning. Come prepared — facilities are basic. The community is warm and welcoming.

pois.name: Piha Canyon Abseiling Adventure
pois.short: Waterfall abseiling through a volcanic canyon in the Waitākere Ranges.
pois.commentary: Piha Canyon offers Auckland's most thrilling canyoning experience, descending through a volcanic rock valley with thundering waterfalls. The highlight is abseiling the highest commercially-operated waterfall in the Auckland region — an adrenaline-pumping adventure just 45 minutes from the CBD.

reviews.summary: Incredible adrenaline rush — the waterfall abseil is epic.

pois.name: Cornwall Park & One Tree Hill Domain
pois.short: Vast central park surrounding Maungakiekie/One Tree Hill volcanic cone.
pois.commentary: Cornwall Park and One Tree Hill Domain together form one of Auckland's largest green spaces, centred on the Maungakiekie volcanic cone with its obelisk memorial to Māori. The parks offer walking trails through mature trees, heritage buildings including Acacia Cottage (Auckland's oldest), and panoramic city views from the summit.

reviews.summary: Beautiful park with great views — the volcanic cone walk is a must.

pois.name: Ponsonby Central Food Hall
pois.short: Eclectic food hall and eatery precinct in Auckland's trendy Ponsonby neighbourhood.
pois.commentary: Ponsonby Central is a vibrant indoor-outdoor dining precinct housing independent eateries, a wine bar, artisan butcher, and specialty grocer. The converted heritage building captures Ponsonby's eclectic dining culture in one convenient location.

reviews.summary: Great variety of food in a buzzy atmosphere.

pois.name: North Head / Maungauika Historic Reserve
pois.short: Historic military tunnels and coastal gun emplacements with harbour panoramas.
pois.commentary: North Head / Maungauika has defended Auckland Harbour since the 1880s. Explore underground tunnels, gun emplacements, and disappearing guns built to repel a feared Russian invasion. The summit provides sweeping views across the harbour to Rangitoto Island and the city skyline.

reviews.summary: Fascinating military history with incredible harbour views.

pois.name: Stonyridge Vineyard — Waiheke Island
pois.short: Acclaimed Waiheke Island vineyard producing world-class Bordeaux-style blends.
pois.commentary: Stonyridge is one of Waiheke Island's founding wineries, famous for its Larose — a Bordeaux-style blend consistently rated among New Zealand's finest reds. The Mediterranean-style courtyard and olive groves make for a sophisticated tasting experience with views over the vineyard.

reviews.summary: Outstanding wines in a stunning vineyard setting.

pois.name: Silo Park & Wynyard Quarter
pois.short: Waterfront precinct with art installations, markets, and converted silo cinema.
pois.commentary: Silo Park is Auckland's waterfront cultural hub, built around converted industrial silos that now host a cinema, art installations, and regular Friday night markets. The surrounding Wynyard Quarter has been transformed from working port to vibrant dining and entertainment precinct.

reviews.summary: Vibrant waterfront precinct — the Friday markets are fantastic.

pois.name: Shakespeare Regional Park
pois.short: Coastal farmland park with WWII gun emplacements and Hauraki Gulf views.
pois.commentary: Shakespeare Regional Park on the Whangaparāoa Peninsula combines coastal farmland walks with WWII military heritage. Gun emplacements from the harbour defence system remain, and the cliff-top walking tracks offer sweeping views across the Hauraki Gulf to Tiritiri Matangi Island.

reviews.summary: Beautiful coastal walks with military history — great family outing.

pois.name: Bastion Point / Takaparawhau Memorial
pois.short: Historic Māori land rights protest site with harbour views and a memorial garden.
pois.commentary: Bastion Point was the site of a 507-day Māori land occupation in 1977-78, one of New Zealand's most significant protests for indigenous rights. Today the Michael Joseph Savage Memorial and Ngāti Whātua memorial share the headland, which offers stunning harbour panoramas.

reviews.summary: Deeply significant site with incredible views — an important visit.
